self_report.reflection: Substituting t=u^2 gives I=2∫_0^∞ e^{-a(u^2+u^{-2})}du. Writing u^2+u^{-2}=(u-1/u)^2+2 pulls out e^{-2a}. The u↔1/u symmetry plus v=u-1/u converts the remaining integral to ∫_{-∞}^∞ e^{-av^2}dv = √(π/a). Hence I = √(π/a)·e^{-2a}, matching the claim exactly.
self_report.notes: Standard Putnam 1985 B-5 derivation reproduces the asserted closed form verbatim.
